title: Salmon en Croute

overview.description: Salmon fillets wrapped in puff pastry with a creamy spinach filling and baked until golden. A classic French-inspired dish that's surprisingly simple to make.

ingredients: 250g frozen chopped spinach, thawed and well drained
method: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over a medium heat.
Stir in the drained spinach and cook until any remaining moisture has evaporated.
Remove from the heat and allow to cool slightly.
Mix in the cream cheese, Dijon mustard, dill and parsley.
Season with salt and black pepper.
Cut the pastry into four equal pieces.
Spread a layer of the spinach mixture onto each piece of pastry.
Place a salmon fillet on top of the filling.
Fold the pastry over the salmon and seal the edges well.
Transfer to a lined baking tray with the seam underneath.
Brush all over with beaten egg.
Score the pastry lightly with a knife to decorate, taking care not to cut right through.
Bake at 180°C fan (200°C conventional) for 25–30 minutes until the pastry is golden and crisp and the salmon is just cooked.

learning.did-you-know: The term 'en croûte' simply means 'in a crust' in French and can refer to many foods baked inside pastry.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to assemble a pastry parcel, prevent a soggy filling and bake salmon so it stays moist.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the family's favourite meals that feels special enough for guests while remaining straightforward to prepare.
chapman-tips: Drain the spinach thoroughly to keep the pastry crisp.
Frozen spinach works just as well as fresh in this recipe.
Don't overcook the salmon—it will continue cooking slightly while resting.
Score only the surface of the pastry to create decoration without letting the filling escape.
notes.batch-cooking: Assemble and freeze unbaked. Bake straight from frozen, adding around 10–15 minutes to the cooking time.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten fresh, but leftovers can be reheated in the oven to help retain the crisp pastry.
